evaluation_inputs.system_description: An Event framework where the application logic and framework are highly inter-dependent. The Main module is part of the framework, and the Event Manager interacts directly with application handlers. It features an Event Queue and a base Handler component extended by standard and application-specific handlers.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Fully developed architecture with class diagrams, component descriptions, and defined component interactions.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: Can the components of the architecture be reused for future projects?
Can the framework be expanded in the future to accommodate a new application or new components?
Will the system handle any input given by the user and handle invalid input?
Is the architecture consistent in behavior?
Can any new application-specific functionality be added to the architecture?
Can the system be modified in short time and cost-effective manner?
Does the architecture perform its event-processing task properly?

hardware_view_description: System accepts input via Command Line Interface (CLI)
Single processor execution implied (sequential event processing)
runtime_constraints: Events must be dispatched in a First-Come-First-Served (FIFO) pattern
System must maintain an 'idle wait' state until input is received
Application logic is embedded within framework components

quantitative_model_parameters.performance_proxy: Number of components involved in a transaction
quantitative_model_parameters.transaction_speed: Qualitative (Slow due to high number of components involved)

questions: Does the system allow unauthorized access?
Does the architecture portray data confidentiality?
Does the architecture process any task in the fastest possible speed?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: No unauthorized access to the system
All operations are processed in fastest possible speed
Any failure should be followed by immediate recovery
User errors in using system should be handled
New demands on system functionality should be accommodated
Major parts of the framework should be re-usable for future architectures
Framework modification is cost-effective, fast and in minimal time
Components in framework interact in a coordinated manner
Framework expansion is possible to allow for more sophisticated options
Implementation allows for execution in different environments
Proper data encapsulation and secure data structures
Flexible implementation using other programming languages
Overall consistent behavior expected from the architecture
Framework should be complete, clear and perform exactly as required
Hook up multiple applications to the framework at same time
Change interface from command-driven to window-based

business_goals: Provide an Event framework to be used by an application
Handle and process events through component interaction
Allow application components to utilize the framework services
technical_constraints: Must handle system termination via STOP handler
Must maintain system in idle state until input is received

architectural_approaches.description: Core architectural style where the system responds to events through an Event Manager that dispatches events from an Event Queue to registered handlers. The framework uses a single Event Manager and Event Queue with FIFO event processing pattern.

addressed_attributes.rationale: FIFO event dispatching ensures predictable, ordered event processing which enhances system reliability and consistency
Centralized event management through a single Event Manager and Event Queue provides a unified, coherent architectural model
Event-driven model enables responsive system behavior through event-based interaction patterns

architectural_approaches.description: Uses a base Handler component that is extended by both standard and application-specific handlers. This extension mechanism allows for customization while maintaining a common interface and framework structure.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Base Handler extension mechanism allows for different handler implementations to be plugged into the framework, supporting system variability
New handlers can be added by extending the base Handler component without modifying the core framework, improving modifiability
Common base Handler ensures all handlers follow consistent patterns and interfaces

architectural_approaches.description: Application logic is embedded within framework components, with the Main module being part of the framework. This creates tight coupling between framework and application layers.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Direct integration of application logic with framework enables efficient event handling and processing
Tight coupling ensures controlled interaction between framework and application components, reducing integration risks

architectural_approaches.description: Events are processed sequentially on a single processor with FIFO dispatching. System maintains idle wait state until input is received via Command Line Interface.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Sequential processing eliminates concurrency issues and race conditions, ensuring deterministic behavior
Simple, linear processing model is easy to understand and reason about

children.refinement: Add new event handler without modifying framework core
children.scenario_description: Add a new event handler to the framework without modifying core Event Manager or Event Queue components. Implementation time < 4 hours, zero changes to framework core.
Change interface from command-driven to window-based. Modification completed in < 2 days without breaking existing handlers.
Reuse major framework components (Event Manager, Event Queue, Handler base) in a new application domain. > 70% of framework components reusable without modification.
Execute framework in different runtime environments (different OS or hardware platforms). Port to new environment in < 1 week with < 10% code changes.
Application-specific handlers can be replaced without modifying framework Event Manager or Event Queue components. Supports clean framework-application separation.
System handles invalid user input without crashing or corrupting event queue. 100% of invalid inputs handled gracefully, system remains operational.
Components interact in coordinated manner maintaining FIFO event processing order. Zero out-of-order event processing under normal load.
When a Handler throws exception during event processing, Event Manager must isolate failure, log error, and continue processing next event without Queue corruption.
Process events at fastest possible speed given single-processor constraint. Event processing latency < 100ms per event for typical handler complexity.
Under high event volume (>100 events/sec), system must maintain FIFO ordering while keeping queue depth <50 events. Sequential processing must not create bottleneck.
Framework exhibits consistent architectural behavior across all event types and handlers. All handlers follow uniform interaction pattern with Event Manager.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer needs to add a new event handler to the framework without modifying core Event Manager or Event Queue components. The new handler must extend the base Handler component, register with the Event Manager, and integrate with the FIFO event dispatching mechanism. Implementation time must be less than 4 hours with zero changes to framework core components.

The system interface must be changed from command-driven (Command Line Interface) to window-based GUI. This modification must be completed in less than 2 days without breaking existing handlers or disrupting the Event Manager and Event Queue interaction patterns. The FIFO event processing order must be preserved after the interface change.

Major framework components (Event Manager, Event Queue, Handler base class) must be reused in a new application domain. More than 70% of framework components should be reusable without modification. The Handler-Based Component Model extension mechanism must support new application-specific handlers while preserving the core event-driven architecture.

The framework must be ported to execute in different runtime environments including different operating systems or hardware platforms. The port to a new environment must be completed in less than 1 week with less than 10% code changes. The Event Manager, Event Queue, and Handler components must remain functionally equivalent across platforms.

Application-specific handlers must be replaceable without modifying framework Event Manager or Event Queue components. The Framework-Application Interdependency Model must support clean separation where application logic can be isolated from framework core, enabling handler replacement while maintaining the centralized event management architecture.

The system receives invalid user input through the Command Line Interface during normal operations. The system must handle 100% of invalid inputs gracefully without crashing or corrupting the Event Queue. The Event Manager must continue processing subsequent valid events, and the FIFO ordering must be maintained despite invalid input attempts.

During normal system operation, multiple events are queued in the Event Queue and dispatched to registered handlers. Components must interact in a coordinated manner maintaining strict FIFO event processing order. Zero out-of-order event processing is acceptable under normal load conditions. The Sequential Event Processing Pattern must ensure deterministic, ordered execution.

A Handler throws an exception during event processing while handling an event dispatched from the Event Queue. The Event Manager must isolate the failure, log the error with sufficient diagnostic information, and continue processing the next event in the queue without Event Queue corruption or system crash. The framework must demonstrate resilience to handler-level failures.

Events are processed at the fastest possible speed given the single-processor constraint and Sequential Event Processing Pattern. Event processing latency must be less than 100ms per event for typical handler complexity. The Event Manager must efficiently dispatch events from the Event Queue to handlers while maintaining FIFO ordering and minimizing overhead.

The system experiences high event volume exceeding 100 events per second during peak load conditions. The system must maintain FIFO ordering while keeping Event Queue depth below 50 events. The Sequential Event Processing Pattern must not create a bottleneck that causes unbounded queue growth or event loss. Handler processing efficiency is critical to throughput.

The framework must exhibit consistent architectural behavior across all event types and handlers. All handlers, whether standard or application-specific, must follow a uniform interaction pattern with the Event Manager. The Event-Driven Architecture with centralized Event Manager and Event Queue must provide a unified, coherent architectural model that is easy to understand and reason about.

scenario_reference.text: A developer needs to add a new event handler to the framework without modifying core Event Manager or Event Queue components. The new handler must extend the base Handler component, register with the Event Manager, and integrate with the FIFO event dispatching mechanism. Implementation time must be less than 4 hours with zero changes to framework core components.

analysis_records.environment: Development environment, framework in stable state
analysis_records.stimulus: Developer request to add new event handler functionality
analysis_records.response_measure: Implementation time < 4 hours, zero changes to framework core (Event Manager, Event Queue)
architectural_decisions: Handler-Based Component Model with base Handler extension mechanism
Event Manager maintains handler registry for dynamic handler registration
FIFO event dispatching through centralized Event Queue
analysis_records.reasoning: The Handler-Based Component Model enables new handlers to be added by extending the base Handler class without modifying Event Manager or Event Queue. However, the Framework-Application Interdependency Model (approach 3) creates tight coupling that may require framework modifications if handler registration or event routing logic needs changes. The 4-hour implementation target is achievable only if the handler extension interface is well-defined and stable.

risks.description: Framework-Application tight coupling may require core modifications for complex handler integration
risks.rationale: The Framework-Application Interdependency Model embeds application logic within framework components. If new handler requires specialized event routing or priority handling, Event Manager core may need modification, violating the zero-change requirement.

risks.description: Handler registration mechanism not explicitly defined in architectural approaches
risks.rationale: While Handler extension is documented, the dynamic registration mechanism with Event Manager is not detailed. Missing registration API or complex registration logic could exceed 4-hour implementation time.

non_risks.description: Base Handler extension mechanism supports new handler addition
non_risks.rationale: Handler-Based Component Model explicitly provides base Handler extension, enabling new handlers to be created without modifying existing framework components, supporting the modifiability goal.

non_risks.description: FIFO event dispatching automatically integrates new handlers
non_risks.rationale: Sequential Event Processing Pattern with FIFO dispatching means new handlers automatically participate in event processing once registered, requiring no changes to dispatching logic.

conflicting_attributes: Functionality (positive: tight integration enables efficient event handling)
Modifiability (negative: tight coupling increases modification complexity)

scenario_reference.text: The system interface must be changed from command-driven (Command Line Interface) to window-based GUI. This modification must be completed in less than 2 days without breaking existing handlers or disrupting the Event Manager and Event Queue interaction patterns. The FIFO event processing order must be preserved after the interface change.

analysis_records.environment: Production system with existing handlers and event processing logic
analysis_records.stimulus: Requirement to change user interface paradigm from CLI to GUI
analysis_records.response_measure: Modification completed in < 2 days, existing handlers unaffected, FIFO order preserved
architectural_decisions: Event-Driven Architecture with centralized Event Manager and Event Queue
Sequential Event Processing Pattern with FIFO dispatching
Framework-Application Interdependency Model with Main module in framework
analysis_records.reasoning: The Event-Driven Architecture should isolate UI changes from event processing logic. However, the Framework-Application Interdependency Model places Main module within the framework, potentially coupling UI initialization to framework core. If CLI input handling is embedded in Event Manager or Main module, extracting it for GUI replacement could require extensive framework modifications, exceeding the 2-day target. FIFO preservation depends on whether GUI event generation maintains sequential event queue insertion.

risks.description: Main module framework placement couples UI initialization to framework core
risks.rationale: Framework-Application Interdependency Model places Main in framework. If CLI initialization is embedded in Main or Event Manager, replacing with GUI may require framework core changes, risking 2-day deadline and handler stability.

risks.description: GUI event generation may not preserve FIFO ordering without architectural changes
risks.rationale: CLI provides sequential input naturally. GUI with multiple windows/widgets may generate concurrent events. Sequential Event Processing Pattern may require event serialization layer, adding complexity beyond 2-day scope.

risks.description: Existing handlers may have CLI-specific dependencies
risks.rationale: If handlers directly interact with CLI (e.g., reading stdin), they will break when CLI is removed. Framework-Application tight coupling increases likelihood of such dependencies.

non_risks.description: Event-Driven Architecture isolates event source from processing
non_risks.rationale: Event-Driven Architecture with Event Manager and Event Queue should decouple event source (UI) from handlers. If properly implemented, UI change should not affect handlers.

non_risks.description: FIFO Event Queue mechanism is UI-agnostic
non_risks.rationale: Event Queue FIFO dispatching operates independently of event source. As long as GUI inserts events into queue sequentially, FIFO order is preserved.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Degree of UI coupling to Event Manager and Main module

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (positive: tight integration ensures controlled interaction)
Modifiability (negative: framework placement of Main couples UI to core)

scenario_reference.text: Major framework components (Event Manager, Event Queue, Handler base class) must be reused in a new application domain. More than 70% of framework components should be reusable without modification. The Handler-Based Component Model extension mechanism must support new application-specific handlers while preserving the core event-driven architecture.

analysis_records.environment: New application domain with different functional requirements
analysis_records.stimulus: Request to reuse framework in new application context
analysis_records.response_measure: > 70% of framework components reusable without modification
architectural_decisions: Event-Driven Architecture with single Event Manager and Event Queue
Handler-Based Component Model with base Handler extension
Framework-Application Interdependency Model with embedded application logic
analysis_records.reasoning: The Event-Driven Architecture and Handler-Based Component Model are designed for reuse. Event Manager, Event Queue, and base Handler should be domain-agnostic. However, the Framework-Application Interdependency Model embeds application logic within framework, reducing reusability. If application-specific logic is tightly coupled to Event Manager or Main module, these components will require modification for new domains, potentially falling below the 70% reusability threshold.

risks.description: Framework-Application tight coupling reduces component reusability below 70% threshold
risks.rationale: Framework-Application Interdependency Model embeds application logic in framework. If Event Manager, Main, or Event Queue contain application-specific code, they cannot be reused without modification, reducing reusability percentage.

risks.description: Single Event Manager and Event Queue design may not fit all application domains
risks.rationale: Event-Driven Architecture uses single Event Manager and Event Queue. Some domains may require multiple event streams, priority queues, or distributed event processing, necessitating architectural changes beyond 30% modification budget.

non_risks.description: Handler-Based Component Model supports domain-specific customization
non_risks.rationale: Base Handler extension mechanism allows new application domains to implement custom handlers without modifying framework core, directly supporting variability goal.

non_risks.description: Event-Driven Architecture is domain-agnostic pattern
non_risks.rationale: Event Manager and Event Queue implement generic event-driven pattern applicable across domains. FIFO dispatching and handler registration are universal mechanisms.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Proportion of application-specific logic embedded in framework
Event Manager and Event Queue genericity

conflicting_attributes: Functionality (positive: embedded logic enables efficient domain-specific processing)
Variability (negative: embedded logic reduces framework reusability across domains)

tradeoff_points.parameter: Event Manager and Event Queue design simplicity
conflicting_attributes: Conceptual Integrity (positive: single Event Manager/Queue provides unified model)
Variability (negative: single-stream design may not fit multi-stream domains)

scenario_reference.text: The framework must be ported to execute in different runtime environments including different operating systems or hardware platforms. The port to a new environment must be completed in less than 1 week with less than 10% code changes. The Event Manager, Event Queue, and Handler components must remain functionally equivalent across platforms.

analysis_records.stimulus: Request to port framework to new runtime environment
analysis_records.response_measure: Port completed in < 1 week with < 10% code changes, functional equivalence maintained
architectural_decisions: Event-Driven Architecture with Event Manager and Event Queue
Sequential Event Processing Pattern on single processor
Handler-Based Component Model
analysis_records.reasoning: Cross-platform portability depends on minimizing platform-specific dependencies. Event-Driven Architecture logic (event dispatching, handler registration) should be platform-independent. However, Sequential Event Processing Pattern assumes single processor, which may have platform-specific threading or process models. CLI in Main module likely uses platform-specific I/O. If these dependencies are isolated, 10% code change is achievable; if scattered throughout framework, portability is at risk.

risks.description: CLI and I/O dependencies may exceed 10% code change budget
risks.rationale: Command Line Interface in Main module uses platform-specific I/O (stdin/stdout). Porting to different OS may require significant I/O layer rewrite, potentially exceeding 10% threshold if not properly abstracted.

risks.description: Single-processor assumption may require concurrency model changes on multi-core platforms
risks.rationale: Sequential Event Processing Pattern assumes single processor. Modern platforms are multi-core. If framework has platform-specific threading or process management, porting may require concurrency model changes beyond 10% budget.

non_risks.description: Event-Driven Architecture core logic is platform-independent
non_risks.rationale: Event Manager, Event Queue, and handler dispatching use standard algorithmic logic (FIFO queue, handler registry) without inherent platform dependencies, supporting portability.

non_risks.description: Handler-Based Component Model is platform-agnostic
non_risks.rationale: Base Handler extension and handler registration mechanisms use object-oriented patterns that are portable across platforms supporting the implementation language.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Degree of platform-specific I/O and concurrency abstraction
Proportion of framework code dependent on OS-specific APIs

conflicting_attributes: Conceptual Integrity (positive: simple single-processor model is easy to understand)
Variability (negative: single-processor assumption may not port well to multi-core platforms)

scenario_reference.text: Application-specific handlers must be replaceable without modifying framework Event Manager or Event Queue components. The Framework-Application Interdependency Model must support clean separation where application logic can be isolated from framework core, enabling handler replacement while maintaining the centralized event management architecture.

analysis_records.environment: Production system requiring handler replacement or upgrade
analysis_records.response_measure: Handler replacement without Event Manager or Event Queue modification, clean framework-application separation
architectural_decisions: Handler-Based Component Model with base Handler extension
Framework-Application Interdependency Model with embedded application logic
Event-Driven Architecture with centralized Event Manager
analysis_records.reasoning: This scenario directly challenges the Framework-Application Interdependency Model. The architectural approach states application logic is embedded within framework components, creating tight coupling. This contradicts the requirement for clean separation and handler replaceability. Handler-Based Component Model supports extension, but if application-specific handlers have dependencies on framework internals or if Event Manager contains application-specific routing logic, clean separation is not achievable without architectural refactoring.

risks.description: Framework-Application tight coupling prevents clean handler isolation and replacement
risks.rationale: Framework-Application Interdependency Model explicitly embeds application logic within framework. This architectural decision directly conflicts with clean separation requirement, making handler replacement without framework modification difficult or impossible.

risks.description: Event Manager may contain application-specific event routing logic
risks.rationale: If Event Manager contains application-specific logic for routing events to particular handlers (beyond generic registration), replacing handlers may require Event Manager modification, violating the scenario requirement.

risks.description: Handler dependencies on framework internals prevent clean replacement
risks.rationale: Tight coupling may cause application-specific handlers to depend on framework internal APIs or data structures. Replacing handlers would then require ensuring new handlers maintain these dependencies, complicating replacement.

non_risks.description: Handler-Based Component Model provides extension interface
non_risks.rationale: Base Handler extension mechanism provides a defined interface for creating handlers. If this interface is stable and application-agnostic, new handlers can be created to replace old ones.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Degree of application logic embedding in framework
Handler interface stability and abstraction level

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (positive: tight coupling ensures controlled interaction, reducing integration risks)
Variability (negative: tight coupling prevents clean handler isolation and replacement)

scenario_reference.text: The system receives invalid user input through the Command Line Interface during normal operations. The system must handle 100% of invalid inputs gracefully without crashing or corrupting the Event Queue. The Event Manager must continue processing subsequent valid events, and the FIFO ordering must be maintained despite invalid input attempts.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with user interaction via CLI
analysis_records.stimulus: Invalid user input received through CLI
analysis_records.response_measure: 100% graceful handling, no crashes, no Event Queue corruption, FIFO order maintained
architectural_decisions: Event-Driven Architecture with Event Manager and Event Queue
Sequential Event Processing Pattern with FIFO dispatching
CLI input handling in Main module (Framework-Application model)
analysis_records.reasoning: Reliability requires robust input validation and error handling. The Event-Driven Architecture should isolate input validation from event processing. Invalid input should be rejected before Event Queue insertion, or handled as a special error event. Sequential Event Processing Pattern ensures FIFO order is maintained as long as Event Queue integrity is preserved. The key risk is whether input validation is properly implemented in CLI/Main module before event creation.

risks.description: Input validation logic placement not specified in architectural approaches
risks.rationale: Architectural approaches do not detail where input validation occurs. If validation is missing or improperly placed (e.g., after event creation), invalid input could corrupt Event Queue or cause handler crashes.

non_risks.description: Sequential Event Processing Pattern ensures FIFO order preservation
non_risks.rationale: Sequential processing with FIFO dispatching guarantees event order. As long as Event Queue is not corrupted, FIFO order is maintained regardless of input validity.

non_risks.description: Event-Driven Architecture isolates input handling from event processing
non_risks.rationale: Event Manager and Event Queue separate input reception from event processing. Invalid input can be rejected at input layer without affecting queued events or handlers.

non_risks.description: Centralized Event Manager enables consistent error handling
non_risks.rationale: Single Event Manager provides a central point for implementing error handling policies, ensuring consistent treatment of invalid inputs across all event types.

scenario_reference.text: During normal system operation, multiple events are queued in the Event Queue and dispatched to registered handlers. Components must interact in a coordinated manner maintaining strict FIFO event processing order. Zero out-of-order event processing is acceptable under normal load conditions. The Sequential Event Processing Pattern must ensure deterministic, ordered execution.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with multiple queued events
analysis_records.stimulus: Multiple events queued and dispatched to handlers
analysis_records.response_measure: Zero out-of-order event processing, strict FIFO order maintained
architectural_decisions: Event-Driven Architecture with single Event Manager and Event Queue
Sequential Event Processing Pattern with FIFO dispatching
Handler-Based Component Model for event handling
analysis_records.reasoning: The Sequential Event Processing Pattern with FIFO dispatching is explicitly designed to ensure ordered execution. Single Event Manager and Event Queue eliminate concurrency issues. As long as Event Queue implements FIFO correctly and Event Manager dispatches sequentially, zero out-of-order processing is guaranteed. The architecture directly supports this reliability requirement.

non_risks.description: Sequential Event Processing Pattern guarantees FIFO order
non_risks.rationale: Sequential processing on single processor with FIFO dispatching eliminates race conditions and ensures deterministic, ordered execution. Architecture directly satisfies zero out-of-order requirement.

non_risks.description: Single Event Manager and Event Queue eliminate concurrency issues
non_risks.rationale: Centralized Event Manager with single Event Queue prevents multiple dispatch threads or concurrent queue access, ensuring strict FIFO ordering.

non_risks.description: Handler-Based Component Model does not affect event ordering
non_risks.rationale: Handlers are invoked sequentially by Event Manager. Handler implementation does not affect FIFO dispatching order, isolating handler logic from ordering concerns.

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (positive: sequential processing ensures deterministic FIFO ordering)
Performance (negative: sequential processing limits throughput under high load)

scenario_reference.text: A Handler throws an exception during event processing while handling an event dispatched from the Event Queue. The Event Manager must isolate the failure, log the error with sufficient diagnostic information, and continue processing the next event in the queue without Event Queue corruption or system crash. The framework must demonstrate resilience to handler-level failures.

analysis_records.stimulus: Handler throws exception during event processing
analysis_records.response_measure: Failure isolated, error logged, next event processed, no Queue corruption, no crash
architectural_decisions: Event-Driven Architecture with Event Manager dispatching to handlers
Handler-Based Component Model with base Handler extension
Sequential Event Processing Pattern
analysis_records.reasoning: Reliability requires Event Manager to implement exception handling around handler invocations. Event Manager must catch handler exceptions, log diagnostics, and continue FIFO dispatching. The Handler-Based Component Model should define error handling contracts. If Event Manager lacks exception handling, handler failures will propagate and crash the system. Sequential processing simplifies error recovery since only one handler executes at a time.

risks.description: Exception handling mechanism not specified in Event Manager architecture
risks.rationale: Architectural approaches do not detail Event Manager exception handling. If Event Manager does not wrap handler invocations in try-catch blocks, handler exceptions will propagate and crash the system, violating reliability requirement.

risks.description: Error logging mechanism not defined in architectural approaches
risks.rationale: Scenario requires error logging with diagnostic information. If logging mechanism is not architected into Event Manager or framework, error isolation may occur but diagnostics will be lost, hindering debugging.

non_risks.description: Sequential processing simplifies exception recovery
non_risks.rationale: Sequential Event Processing Pattern means only one handler executes at a time. Exception in one handler does not affect Event Queue state or other handlers, simplifying isolation and recovery.

non_risks.description: Event Queue is isolated from handler execution
non_risks.rationale: Event-Driven Architecture separates Event Queue from handler execution. Handler exceptions occur after event dequeue, so Queue integrity is naturally preserved.

scenario_reference.text: Events are processed at the fastest possible speed given the single-processor constraint and Sequential Event Processing Pattern. Event processing latency must be less than 100ms per event for typical handler complexity. The Event Manager must efficiently dispatch events from the Event Queue to handlers while maintaining FIFO ordering and minimizing overhead.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with typical handler complexity
architectural_decisions: Sequential Event Processing Pattern on single processor
Event-Driven Architecture with Event Manager and Event Queue
Handler-Based Component Model
analysis_records.reasoning: Performance depends on Event Manager dispatch efficiency and handler execution time. Sequential processing eliminates concurrency overhead but limits parallelism. 100ms latency budget must cover Event Queue dequeue, Event Manager dispatch, and handler execution. If Event Manager has minimal overhead and handlers are efficient, 100ms is achievable. However, 'typical handler complexity' is undefined, creating uncertainty.

risks.description: Handler execution time not bounded by architecture
risks.rationale: Handler-Based Component Model allows arbitrary handler implementations. If handlers perform complex operations (I/O, computation), execution time may exceed 100ms budget. Architecture does not enforce handler performance constraints.

risks.description: Event Manager dispatch overhead not quantified
risks.rationale: Architectural approaches do not specify Event Manager implementation efficiency. If dispatch involves complex handler lookup, event copying, or logging, overhead may consume significant portion of 100ms budget.

non_risks.description: Sequential processing eliminates concurrency overhead
non_risks.rationale: Sequential Event Processing Pattern avoids thread synchronization, context switching, and lock contention, minimizing processing overhead and supporting low latency.

non_risks.description: FIFO Event Queue enables efficient dequeue operations
non_risks.rationale: FIFO queue data structure supports O(1) dequeue operations, minimizing Event Queue overhead in dispatch path.

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (positive: sequential processing ensures deterministic behavior)
Performance (negative: single-processor constraint limits throughput and parallelism)

scenario_reference.text: The system experiences high event volume exceeding 100 events per second during peak load conditions. The system must maintain FIFO ordering while keeping Event Queue depth below 50 events. The Sequential Event Processing Pattern must not create a bottleneck that causes unbounded queue growth or event loss. Handler processing efficiency is critical to throughput.

analysis_records.environment: Peak load with high event volume (>100 events/sec)
analysis_records.stimulus: High event arrival rate exceeding 100 events/sec
analysis_records.response_measure: FIFO ordering maintained, Event Queue depth < 50 events, no unbounded growth, no event loss
architectural_decisions: Sequential Event Processing Pattern on single processor
Event-Driven Architecture with single Event Manager and Event Queue
Handler-Based Component Model
analysis_records.reasoning: This scenario creates a critical performance bottleneck. At 100 events/sec, average handler processing time must be < 10ms to prevent queue growth. Sequential processing on single processor means handlers execute serially, limiting throughput. If handler processing time exceeds event arrival rate, queue depth will grow unbounded. The 50-event queue depth limit will be violated unless handlers are extremely efficient or event arrival is throttled. This is a high-risk scenario for the architecture.

risks.description: Sequential processing creates throughput bottleneck under high load
risks.rationale: Sequential Event Processing Pattern on single processor limits throughput to 1/(handler_time). At 100 events/sec, handler time must be <10ms. If handlers require I/O, computation, or complex logic, this is unachievable, causing unbounded queue growth and violating 50-event depth limit.

risks.description: No backpressure or flow control mechanism defined
risks.rationale: Architectural approaches do not include backpressure or event throttling. If event arrival exceeds processing capacity, queue will grow unbounded until memory exhaustion or system crash, violating reliability.

risks.description: Handler efficiency not architecturally enforced
risks.rationale: Handler-Based Component Model allows arbitrary handler implementations. No architectural constraints ensure handlers meet <10ms processing time required for 100 events/sec throughput.

non_risks.description: FIFO ordering naturally maintained by Sequential Event Processing
non_risks.rationale: Sequential processing guarantees FIFO order even under high load, satisfying ordering requirement.

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (positive: FIFO ordering guaranteed)
Performance (negative: single-processor bottleneck limits throughput to ~10ms per event at 100 events/sec)
Performance (positive: bounded queue prevents memory exhaustion)
Reliability (negative: bounded queue may require event dropping, causing event loss)

scenario_reference.text: The framework must exhibit consistent architectural behavior across all event types and handlers. All handlers, whether standard or application-specific, must follow a uniform interaction pattern with the Event Manager. The Event-Driven Architecture with centralized Event Manager and Event Queue must provide a unified, coherent architectural model that is easy to understand and reason about.

analysis_records.environment: Framework with multiple event types and handlers
analysis_records.stimulus: Evaluation of architectural consistency across components
analysis_records.response_measure: Uniform handler interaction pattern, unified architectural model, easy to understand
architectural_decisions: Event-Driven Architecture with single Event Manager and Event Queue
Handler-Based Component Model with base Handler extension
Sequential Event Processing Pattern with FIFO dispatching
analysis_records.reasoning: Conceptual integrity is strongly supported by the architecture. Single Event Manager and Event Queue provide a unified event processing model. Handler-Based Component Model with base Handler ensures all handlers follow consistent interface. Sequential Event Processing Pattern provides simple, deterministic execution model. These decisions create a coherent, understandable architecture with minimal conceptual complexity.

non_risks.description: Single Event Manager and Event Queue provide unified architectural model
non_risks.rationale: Centralized Event Manager and Event Queue create a single, consistent event processing pattern. All events flow through the same mechanism, ensuring uniform behavior and easy comprehension.

non_risks.description: Handler-Based Component Model ensures uniform handler interaction
non_risks.rationale: Base Handler extension mechanism enforces consistent interface for all handlers. Standard and application-specific handlers follow identical registration and invocation patterns with Event Manager.

non_risks.description: Sequential Event Processing Pattern provides simple, deterministic model
non_risks.rationale: Sequential processing with FIFO dispatching is conceptually simple and easy to reason about. Eliminates concurrency complexity, making architectural behavior predictable and understandable.

tradeoff_points.parameter: Architectural simplicity (single Event Manager/Queue, sequential processing)
conflicting_attributes: Conceptual Integrity (positive: simple model is easy to understand and reason about)
Performance (negative: simple model limits throughput and scalability)
